title: Stick Game
description:
Consider a game where two players remove sticks from a heap. Players move alternately, and the player who removes the last stick wins.

A set P={p1,p2,...,pk} determines the allowed moves. For example, if P={1,3,4}, a player may remove 1, 3 or 4 sticks.

Task: For each number of sticks 1..n, determine if the first player has a winning (W) or losing (L) position, assuming optimal play.

Input:
- First line: two integers n and k (number of sticks and number of allowed moves).
- Second line: k distinct integers p1..pk that describe the allowed moves. One of them is 1.

Output:
Print a string of n characters where the i-th character is W if i sticks is a winning position for the first player, otherwise L.

mcqChain.questionText: With P = {1,3,4}, is the position with 1 stick winning or losing for the player to move?
mcqChain.explanation: You can remove 1 stick and win immediately. So 1 is W.

wrongExplanations.1: If you can remove 1, the current player wins from 1.
wrongExplanations.2: k only affects which moves are allowed, but here 1 is allowed.
wrongExplanations.3: It is determined: remove 1 and win.

mcqChain.hint: Try the immediate move that ends the game.

mcqChain.questionText: With P = {1,3,4}, is the position with 2 sticks winning or losing?

options.label: Winning (W): remove 1 to leave 1 which is L
Losing (L): any move leaves 1, and the opponent wins
Losing (L): no moves are allowed from 2

mcqChain.explanation: Only move is to remove 1 leaving 1, which is W for the next player, so 2 is L.

wrongExplanations.0: Leaving 1 is not losing; it's winning for the opponent.
wrongExplanations.2: You cannot remove more sticks than exist.
wrongExplanations.3: Removing 1 is allowed; so there is a move.

mcqChain.hint: Consider the only legal move from 2.

mcqChain.questionText: With P = {1,3,4}, is the position with 3 sticks winning or losing?

options.label: Losing (L): remove 1 to 2 which is L
Losing (L): any move leaves a winning position

mcqChain.explanation: You can remove 3 and end the game, so 3 is W.

wrongExplanations.0: Removing 1 to 2 leaves L, which would make 3 W, not L.
wrongExplanations.3: You cannot remove more sticks than exist.

mcqChain.hint: Is there a move that takes all sticks?

mcqChain.questionText: If 1 is always in P, does that mean all positions with i ≥ 1 are winning?

options.label: No, some positions can still be losing (e.g., i=2 for {1,3,4})
Yes, because you can always move

mcqChain.explanation: Having a move doesn’t guarantee a win; if all moves lead to wins for the opponent, the position is losing.

wrongExplanations.1: Having a move isn’t enough; the resulting position matters.
wrongExplanations.2: Parity doesn’t generally determine outcomes here.

mcqChain.hint: Recall the result for i=2 with {1,3,4}.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the status of the position with 0 sticks for the player to move?

options.label: Winning (W): the game is already over in your favor
Losing (L): you have no legal move
Winning (W): you pass and the opponent loses

mcqChain.explanation: No legal moves means the current player loses from 0 sticks.

wrongExplanations.0: There’s no way to win immediately; no sticks remain.
wrongExplanations.2: Passing is not allowed; the player to move must move.

mcqChain.hint: Who moves when there are no sticks?

mcqChain.questionText: Would exploring all possible move sequences up to n (n ≤ 10^6) be feasible?

options.label: No, exploring all sequences is too slow for n up to 10^6
Yes, recursion over all sequences will be fine

mcqChain.explanation: Enumerating all sequences explodes combinatorially and won’t fit in time for n up to 10^6.

wrongExplanations.1: The branching factor makes brute forcing too large.
wrongExplanations.3: Even with small k, depth n makes it infeasible.

mcqChain.hint: Consider the number of paths in a tree of depth ~n with branching up to k.

mcqChain.questionText: Let status[i] denote W/L for i sticks. Which rule correctly determines status[i] from smaller positions?

options.label: status[i] is W if there exists p ≤ i with status[i − p] = L; else L
status[i] is W if all p ≤ i have status[i − p] = W; else L
status[i] is L if there exists p ≤ i with status[i − p] = W; else W
status[i] equals status[i − 1] if 1 ∈ P; otherwise L

mcqChain.explanation: A position is winning if you can move to a losing position; otherwise it’s losing.

wrongExplanations.1: Requiring all moves to lead to W is too strong; one losing child is enough.
wrongExplanations.2: This is the negated condition; it inverts the logic.

mcqChain.hint: Try to move to an L position; if you can, you force a win.

mcqChain.questionText: In what order should we compute status[i] so that needed smaller values are already known?

options.label: Only for i that are multiples of min(P)
In random order; the rule doesn’t depend on order

mcqChain.explanation: Each status[i] depends on smaller indices, so increasing order ensures dependencies are ready.

wrongExplanations.1: Decreasing order won’t have status[i − p] known yet.
wrongExplanations.2: All i need to be computed, not just multiples.

mcqChain.hint: status[i] refers to status[i − p].

mcqChain.questionText: What data structure efficiently stores status[0..n] for fast lookups while computing?

options.label: An array (or byte array) of length n+1 storing 0/1 for L/W
A set of winning positions only
A dictionary mapping i to status computed on demand
Recompute status[i] from scratch whenever needed

mcqChain.explanation: A contiguous array allows O(1) access for status[i−p] during computation.

wrongExplanations.1: You also need to identify losing positions quickly.
wrongExplanations.3: Recomputing repeatedly is too slow for n up to 10^6.

mcqChain.hint: We’ll check many status[i−p] values; random access helps.

mcqChain.questionText: What should status[0] be initialized to?

options.label: Winning (1), since no sticks means you already won
Losing (0), since no move is possible
Uninitialized; it will not be used

mcqChain.explanation: No move is possible at 0, so it’s losing for the player to move.

wrongExplanations.0: Winning requires making the last move; at 0 it’s your turn with no move.
wrongExplanations.2: status[0] is needed (e.g., when p equals i).

mcqChain.hint: From 0, the current player has no move.

mcqChain.questionText: While checking moves p for a given i, how should we handle p > i?

options.label: Skip such p since i − p would be negative
Consider it as an automatic losing move

mcqChain.explanation: You cannot remove more sticks than exist; ignore moves p > i.

wrongExplanations.1: i − p being negative is not a valid position.
wrongExplanations.2: It’s not a valid move, so it shouldn’t affect status.

mcqChain.hint: Only legal moves matter.

mcqChain.questionText: For P={1,3,4}, what is the sequence for i=1..5?
mcqChain.explanation: Computed stepwise: 1=W, 2=L, 3=W, 4=W, 5=W.

wrongExplanations.1: 2 is L because it only moves to 1 (W).

mcqChain.hint: Use the rule: W if any move goes to L; else L.

mcqChain.questionText: While checking p for status[i], can we stop early if we find a move to a losing position?

options.label: Yes, once any dp[i−p] is losing, status[i] is winning
No, we must check all moves to be sure
Stop only if p is the largest move

mcqChain.explanation: One losing child guarantees the current position is winning.

wrongExplanations.1: Checking all moves isn’t needed once a losing child is found.

mcqChain.hint: The condition uses “exists” a losing child.

mcqChain.questionText: When is it valid to break the inner loop upon encountering p > i?

options.label: Only if moves are sorted in increasing order
Always, because p > i means no further moves are valid
Never; we must scan all moves

mcqChain.explanation: If moves are sorted ascending, all subsequent p are ≥ current p, hence also > i.

wrongExplanations.1: Without sorting, larger p might appear later; you could skip valid smaller p.
wrongExplanations.3: Breaking saves time once further p can’t be valid.

mcqChain.hint: Think about move order and why it matters for early termination.

mcqChain.questionText: Choose a fast way to read all input tokens.
mcqChain.explanation: Reading the entire input and splitting into tokens is fast and simple.

wrongExplanations.1: Splitlines leaves tokens unseparated within each line.
wrongExplanations.2: Reads only one line; we need both lines.

mcqChain.hint: We want all tokens, not just per line.

mcqChain.questionText: Parse n and k from the token list.

options.label:
it = iter(data)
n = int(next(it)); k = int(next(it))

mcqChain.explanation: Using an iterator and consuming two tokens yields n and k reliably.

wrongExplanations.1: Attempts to unpack all tokens into two variables.
wrongExplanations.3: k is the second token (index 1), not index 2.

mcqChain.hint: Consume exactly two tokens for n and k.
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
def main():
    data = sys.stdin.buffer.read().split()
    it = iter(data)
    n = int(next(it)); k = int(next(it))

mcqChain.questionText: Read the k allowed moves from the tokens.
mcqChain.explanation: Consume k tokens for moves and convert each to int.

wrongExplanations.1: next(it) gives a single token; mapping int over it iterates its bytes/chars.
wrongExplanations.3: You should read k moves, not n.

mcqChain.hint: There are exactly k move values to read.

mcqChain.questionText: Sort the moves so we can break early when p > i.
mcqChain.explanation: In-place sort ascending ensures that after p > i, all further moves are too large.

wrongExplanations.1: sort() returns None; this would set moves to None.
wrongExplanations.2: Descending order breaks the p > i early-exit logic.

mcqChain.hint: We want increasing order.

mcqChain.questionText: Initialize the status storage for 0..n as 0/1 values.
mcqChain.explanation: A bytearray of length n+1 holds 0/1 status for each i, including i=0 and i=n.

wrongExplanations.1: Length n misses index n; we need n+1.
wrongExplanations.2: Length is n, missing the n-th position.

mcqChain.hint: We need indices 0..n inclusive.

mcqChain.questionText: Set the base case value.
mcqChain.explanation: dp[0] should be losing (0).

wrongExplanations.1: 0 sticks is losing, not winning.

mcqChain.hint: From 0 sticks, the player cannot move.

mcqChain.questionText: Write the outer loop over i.
mcqChain.explanation: We need to fill dp[1]..dp[n] inclusive.

wrongExplanations.1: This iterates from 0..n-1 and misses dp[n].
wrongExplanations.3: i is undefined and not incremented.

mcqChain.hint: Indices start at 1 and must include n.
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
for i in range(1, n + 1):
        win = 0

mcqChain.questionText: Iterate moves and skip too-large moves efficiently.
mcqChain.explanation: With moves sorted ascending, p > i means all remaining moves are too large.

wrongExplanations.1: Breaking on p >= i would skip the valid case p == i.
wrongExplanations.3: Descending order breaks the early stopping condition.

mcqChain.hint: We sorted moves ascending for this purpose.

mcqChain.questionText: Fill the winning condition inside the inner loop.
mcqChain.explanation: If any move leads to a losing position, current is winning; set win and break.

wrongExplanations.1: Moving to a winning position doesn’t make current one winning.
wrongExplanations.2: This would never set a win.

mcqChain.hint: We want to detect any move to a losing child.
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
if dp[i - p] == 0:
                win = 1
                break
        dp[i] = win

mcqChain.questionText: Build the output string based on dp.
mcqChain.explanation: Initialize all to 'L', then set 'W' at indices where dp[i] is true. Use i−1 for 0-based list.

wrongExplanations.1: This would never set 'L' where needed.
wrongExplanations.3: Index i is out of range when i == n.

mcqChain.hint: Map i in 1..n to out index i−1.

mcqChain.questionText: Write the output and add the standard entry point.
mcqChain.explanation: Join the list into a string and write it. Then call main via the entry point.

wrongExplanations.1: This prints the Python list representation, not the required string.
wrongExplanations.3: Writing out after main() in global scope won’t work; out is local to main.

mcqChain.hint: Output must be a contiguous string of W/L characters.

mcqChain.questionText: For n=10, k=3, P={1,3,4}, what output string should the program print?
mcqChain.explanation: By stepwise evaluation: 1..10 → W L W W W W L W L W.

wrongExplanations.1: Check positions 6 and 9 carefully.

mcqChain.hint: We computed 1..5 earlier; continue similarly.

mcqChain.questionText: For i=6 with P={1,3,4}, what is status[6] and why?

options.label: W: remove 4 to leave 2, which is L
L: all moves go to W
W: remove 3 to leave 3, which is L
L: remove 1 to leave 5, which is L

mcqChain.explanation: 6−4=2 and 2 is L, so 6 is W.

wrongExplanations.1: There exists a move to L (remove 4).

mcqChain.hint: Check dp[2], dp[3], dp[5] from earlier results.

mcqChain.questionText: If P={1} and n=5, what is the output string?
mcqChain.explanation: Only one move flips W/L alternately starting at 1=W, 2=L, etc.

wrongExplanations.1: Starts with 1=W, not L.
wrongExplanations.3: There is always a move for i≥1.

mcqChain.hint: With only removing 1, the status alternates.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the time complexity of the approach that computes status[1..n] by checking all allowed moves for each i?
mcqChain.explanation: We process n positions and consider up to k moves for each.

wrongExplanations.1: We do more than linear in n; each i checks up to k moves.
wrongExplanations.3: We are not nesting loops over n twice.

mcqChain.hint: Outer loop over i, inner loop over moves.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the extra space complexity of this approach?
mcqChain.explanation: The dp array dominates with size n+1; moves is at most 100.

wrongExplanations.1: We store status for all positions.
wrongExplanations.3: We also store dp for n positions.

mcqChain.hint: We keep status for all 0..n.

mcqChain.questionText: Given 1 ∈ P, which statement is correct?

options.label: For any i > 0, there is at least one legal move
All positions with i ≥ 1 are winning
The pattern of W/L repeats every k positions

mcqChain.explanation: At least removing 1 is always available for i>0; positions can still be losing.

wrongExplanations.1: As seen for i=2, some positions are losing.
wrongExplanations.3: No general periodicity guarantee from the statement.

mcqChain.hint: Legal moves don’t guarantee wins.

mcqChain.questionText: Why prefer a byte array (0/1) for status over a list of Python integers?

options.label: It’s compact and fast for storing 0/1 values at large n
It avoids needing a base case
It allows negative indexing to handle p > i

mcqChain.explanation: bytearray stores one byte per entry and is efficient for large arrays of 0/1.

wrongExplanations.1: Sorting moves is unrelated to status storage.
wrongExplanations.3: We don’t use negative indices; we skip p > i.

mcqChain.hint: n can be up to 10^6; memory matters.

finalCodeSnippets:
data = sys.stdin.buffer.read().split()
    it = iter(data)
    n = int(next(it)); k = int(next(it))

reasoningChain.question: How can we decide whether i sticks is winning or losing using smaller positions?
reasoningChain.insight: A position i is winning if there exists a legal move p ≤ i such that the position i − p is losing; otherwise it is losing.

reasoningChain.question: What base case should we use to start the computation?
reasoningChain.insight: With 0 sticks, the current player has no move, so status[0] is losing.

reasoningChain.question: In what order should we compute statuses to ensure dependencies are ready?
reasoningChain.insight: Compute from i = 1 up to n so that all referenced statuses i − p are already computed.

reasoningChain.question: How do we efficiently skip impossible moves and speed up scanning?
reasoningChain.insight: Sort the move list ascending and break when p > i to avoid checking larger moves.

reasoningChain.question: What data structure should store the statuses efficiently for n up to 10^6?
reasoningChain.insight: Use a contiguous array (e.g., bytearray) of length n+1 storing 0/1 for losing/winning.

reasoningChain.question: How do we produce the final required output format?
reasoningChain.insight: Map status[1..n] to characters: 'W' for winning (1), 'L' for losing (0), then join into a single string.
